title: Covering boards with paper
subtitle: Wrapping binders board cleanly for soft and case-bound journals
excerpt: Covering a board with decorative or plain paper is the visible step in case binding. A well-covered board has no air bubbles, no wrinkled corners, and no visible adhesive seepage. The technique is the same whether the paper is handmade, marbled, or plain cartridge.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on covering. Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), section on cloth and paper covering. Internet Archive scans available.

glossaryTerms.definition: The strip of covering paper or cloth folded over the edge of the board onto the inner face. The turn-in is typically 15 to 20 mm wide.
Compressed grey-board used for book covers. Denser and less prone to warping than cardboard; made in standard thicknesses of 1.5, 2, and 3 mm.

content.text: Covering
binders board

with paper is the outer surface of any hand-bound book with hard covers. The same method works whether the covering paper is marbled, plain, or patterned. The critical variables are consistent glue application, snug
turn-ins
, and pressing under weight while the adhesive dries.
What you need

items.name: Covering paper (at least 30 mm larger on all sides than the board)

attrs.heading: Adhesive and cutting
attrs.body: PVA dries quickly on the paper surface; work one board at a time. Cut against a metal ruler on a cutting mat, hand behind the blade's path.

content.text: Cutting the covering paper
Lay the board on the covering paper. Mark a cut line 20 mm out from each edge of the board. Cut along these lines. The resulting piece is the board size plus 20 mm turn-in on all four sides.

Check the grain direction of the covering paper. The grain should run parallel to the spine edge of the board (typically the long side). Paper covered against the grain will pull the board into a curve as it dries.
Applying the adhesive
Place the covering paper face-down on waste paper. Brush PVA evenly across the whole surface, working from the centre outward. The coat should be thin and even, not pooled at the edges.
Set the board face-down in the centre of the glued paper. Press firmly and slide onto a clean surface. The paper should not be so wet that it stretches noticeably.
Turn the assembly over and smooth the paper from the centre outward with the flat of the hand, then the bone folder, pressing out any trapped air. Work from centre to edges, not from edges in.
Turning in the edges

At each corner, cut diagonally through the covering paper at 45 degrees, leaving a gap of roughly one board-thickness between the cut and the board corner. This creates a clean mitre rather than a bulky layered corner.

Fold the long-side turn-ins first. Apply a thin strip of PVA to the turn-in flap, fold it over the board edge, and press down firmly with the bone folder. Push the paper into the corner where the turn-in meets the board edge.
Fold the short-side turn-ins. Before pressing down, use the bone folder to tuck each mitred corner flap neatly around the board corner. Press firmly.
Stand the board on the covered side and check the turn-ins are flat against the inner face with no bubbles. Any bubble can still be pressed out at this stage with the bone folder.
Pressing

Set the covered board between two clean sheets of silicone-release paper (or plain cartridge) and place under a heavy flat weight (a stack of books, a paving stone) for at least an hour. A book press is ideal. Do not allow the board to dry unconstrained or it will curve toward the paper side as moisture leaves the adhesive.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that appear when covering boards.

items.symptom: Board curves toward the covered side after drying
items.cause: Paper grain running in the wrong direction, or adhesive applied too wet.
items.fix: For a slight curve, dampen the inner face of the board lightly with a damp sponge and press flat overnight. For persistent warp, cover the inside face with a matching paper to balance the tension.

items.symptom: Bubbles appear under the covering paper
items.cause: Air trapped when the board was set down, or adhesive dried on the paper before the board was placed.
items.fix: Work quickly after gluing. A bubble still wet with adhesive can be pressed out. A dry bubble must be slit with the tip of the craft knife and re-glued.

items.cause: Too much paper left at the mitre cut.
items.fix: The mitre cut should leave a gap of one board-thickness between the cut and the corner. Re-cut closer if the paper is thick.
